text: Following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon inserts four robotic trochars and two laparoscopic trochars into the upper abdomen, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon dissects and removes adhesions between the omentum and the gallbladder, clearing the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the large choledochal cyst and carefully dissects it from surrounding tissues, revealing its anatomical position and structure.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum between the duodenum and the choledochal cyst, mobilizing the duodenum away from the cyst wall.
The surgical team focuses on the left side of the hepatic hilum, carefully dissecting to expose the proper hepatic artery and its branches, revealing the vascular structures within the area.
The surgeon meticulously dissects lymphatic tissues away from the hepatic artery, ensuring clear visualization and separation of the artery from surrounding structures.
The proper hepatic artery is clearly visualized, running along the left side of the choledochal cyst.
The surgeon encircles the large choledochal cyst with umbilical tape, securing it for further manipulation.
The surgeon retracts the colloidal cyst superiorly and dissects the inferior pole of the cyst within the pancreatic head, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the cyst wall from the surrounding pancreatic tissues, carefully separating the cyst from the pancreatic head.
The surgeon identifies the abrupt tapering of the cyst, marking the inferior edge of the colloidal cyst within the pancreatic head.
The surgeon ligates and transects the choledochal cyst, with suture ligation applied distally, following the visualization of the proper hepatic artery running along the left side of the cyst.
The surgeon retracts the colloidal cyst superiorly, dissecting the posterior wall from the hepatic hilum.
The surgeon continues dissection superiorly towards the insertion of the cystic duct, following the previous retraction of the cyst.
The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy using a fundus-first approach, beginning the dissection at the gallbladder fundus and proceeding towards the cystic duct.
The surgeon transects the cystic artery, completing the dissection of the gallbladder from the hepatic hilum.
The surgeon maintains the attachment of the cystic duct to the choledochal cyst, ensuring the duct remains connected during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the bifurcation of the bile duct, observing the anatomical division into separate branches.
The surgeon preserves the right hepatic artery, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The superior edge of the choledochal cyst is clearly visualized, highlighting its anatomical position in relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon transects the normal caliber common hepatic duct using an endoscopic stapler, visibly separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon creates a Roux limb measuring approximately forty centimeters in length.
The surgeon transects the jejunum using an endo-GIA stapler, visibly separating the intestinal segment.
The surgeon creates a jejunal jejunostomy using the endo-GIA stapler, followed by closing the stapler enterotomy with sutures.
After creating the Roux limb, the surgeon resects the stapled stump of the common hepatic duct.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the Roux limb, visibly opening the intestinal segment.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy using interrupted fibro-PDS sutures, connecting the hepatic duct to the jejunum.
The surgeon inserts the posterior row of sutures, securing the tissue layers in place.
The surgeon places an anterior row of PDS sutures, completing the suturing process visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the completed anastomosis, which appears circumferentially intact, indicating successful connection of the structures.
The surgeon inspects the surgical site for bowel leaks, finding none, and proceeds to insert a drain into the area.
The surgical field is inspected to ensure hemostasis, with no active bleeding visible.
